title: What I lost when I stopped borrowing books
summary: Reflect on the small but real difference between borrowing books from a library and buying them, and what was lost when one habit replaced the other.
topic: Borrowing books from a library versus buying them
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) on the difference between borrowing books from a library and buying them, and what changed in your reading life when one habit replaced the other. Use the present perfect, the past perfect, a second conditional, and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: Until I was twenty-six I had borrowed almost every book I read from one of three libraries in central Krakow, and then, almost without noticing, I…
If you had asked me at the time whether buying my own books would change my reading life in any deep way, I would have said no, but in fact…
Although owning my books has obvious advantages, what I have come to miss about the library habit is something more difficult to name, which is…

hintVocab.meaningVi: xây dựng tủ sách cá nhân

hintVocab.phrase: to feel the weight of choice
hintVocab.meaningVi: cảm thấy sức nặng của lựa chọn
cam kết với một cuốn sách

hintVocab.phrase: a fresh start with a new book
hintVocab.meaningVi: một khởi đầu mới với một cuốn sách mới

sampleText:
Until I was twenty-six I had borrowed almost every book I read from one of three libraries in central Krakow. The system was simple: I would visit on a Saturday morning, choose three books I genuinely planned to read, and return them three weeks later whether I had finished them or not. The deadline was the discipline. If a book did not interest me after fifty pages, I returned it without guilt; the next reader was waiting. If a book did, I read it carefully, sometimes finishing it on the tram on the way back to the library because I knew I would not be allowed to keep it any longer. Then I got my first salary that allowed me to think of books as something I could own, and I began buying them. That seemed, at the time, like an obvious step up.

If you had asked me whether buying my own books would change my reading life in any deep way, I would have said no, and I would have been wrong. Within a year I had a shelf of unread books beside my bed, organised by intention rather than interest. Each one represented a future version of me who would have time, energy, and the right mood to finish it. Although I was technically reading the same amount, the experience had become heavier. A library book asked one question: did you read this in three weeks? A bought book asked a different one: what kind of person are you that you have owned this for seven months and have not opened it past chapter two? The library had been kind to me in a way I had not understood. It had set deadlines, accepted my failures cheerfully, and never let unread books accumulate as evidence against me.

However, I do not want to overstate this. There are obvious advantages to owning books — being able to underline freely, to lend them to friends, to re-read favourites without queuing. The library habit had its own costs, including the small humiliation of being told a book I wanted was already out. What I have come to miss, though, is something less practical. The library treated reading as a public, time-shared activity in which I was one of many readers passing through a fixed shelf. Buying treats reading as a private, permanent collection that quietly measures me against my own intentions. Both are valid, but they shape the reader differently. These days I have started borrowing again, two books at a time, alongside the ones I own, and the difference in how each kind of book actually gets read is something I am still trying to understand a year into the experiment.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: choose three books I genuinely planned to read
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: chọn ba cuốn sách tôi thực sự có ý định đọc

sampleAnnotations.phrase: return them three weeks later whether I had finished them or not
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: trả lại chúng sau ba tuần bất kể tôi đã đọc xong hay chưa
Hạn chót chính là sự kỷ luật

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect for a habit that ended before the main story
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Until I was twenty-six I had borrowed almost every book I read' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành để diễn đạt một thói quen kéo dài đến một thời điểm cụ thể trong quá khứ, sau đó dừng lại. Đây là cách dùng cổ điển với 'until'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Until I was twenty-six I had borrowed almost every book I read' uses the past perfect to express a habit that continued until a specific past moment and then stopped. This is a classic use with 'until'.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional with embedded prediction
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If you had asked me whether buying my own books would change my reading life in any deep way, I would have said no' kết hợp điều kiện loại 3 với 'would' bên trong mệnh đề 'whether' để diễn đạt một dự đoán giả định.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If you had asked me whether buying my own books would change my reading life in any deep way, I would have said no' combines the third conditional with 'would' inside a 'whether' clause to express a hypothetical prediction.

sampleTranslationVi:
Cho đến khi tôi hai mươi sáu tuổi, tôi đã mượn gần như mọi cuốn sách tôi đọc từ một trong ba thư viện ở trung tâm Krakow. Hệ thống đơn giản: tôi sẽ ghé thăm vào sáng thứ Bảy, chọn ba cuốn sách tôi thực sự có ý định đọc, và trả lại chúng sau ba tuần bất kể tôi đã đọc xong hay chưa. Hạn chót chính là sự kỷ luật. Nếu một cuốn sách không thu hút tôi sau năm mươi trang, tôi trả nó lại mà không cảm thấy tội lỗi; người đọc tiếp theo đang chờ. Nếu một cuốn có, tôi đọc nó cẩn thận, đôi khi đọc xong trên xe điện trên đường trở về thư viện vì tôi biết tôi sẽ không được giữ nó lâu hơn. Rồi tôi nhận được mức lương đầu tiên cho phép tôi nghĩ về sách như điều gì đó tôi có thể sở hữu, và tôi bắt đầu mua chúng. Điều đó có vẻ, lúc đó, như một bước tiến rõ ràng.

Nếu bạn đã hỏi tôi liệu việc mua sách riêng có thay đổi cuộc sống đọc sách của tôi theo cách sâu sắc nào không, tôi đã sẽ nói không, và tôi đã sẽ sai. Trong vòng một năm, tôi có một kệ sách chưa đọc bên cạnh giường, được sắp xếp theo ý định thay vì sự quan tâm. Mỗi cuốn đại diện cho một phiên bản tương lai của tôi sẽ có thời gian, năng lượng và tâm trạng phù hợp để đọc xong. Mặc dù về kỹ thuật tôi đang đọc cùng số lượng, trải nghiệm đã trở nên nặng nề hơn. Một cuốn sách thư viện hỏi một câu: bạn đã đọc cuốn này trong ba tuần chưa? Một cuốn sách mua hỏi một câu khác: bạn là loại người gì mà đã sở hữu cuốn này bảy tháng và chưa mở quá chương hai? Thư viện đã tử tế với tôi theo cách tôi đã không hiểu. Nó đã đặt ra hạn chót, vui vẻ chấp nhận thất bại của tôi, và không bao giờ để sách chưa đọc tích tụ như bằng chứng chống lại tôi.

Tuy nhiên, tôi không muốn nói quá. Có những lợi thế rõ ràng khi sở hữu sách — có thể gạch chân tự do, cho bạn bè mượn, đọc lại những cuốn yêu thích mà không xếp hàng. Thói quen thư viện có chi phí riêng của nó, bao gồm sự bẽ mặt nhỏ khi được nói rằng cuốn sách tôi muốn đã được người khác mượn. Điều tôi đã dần nhớ, dù vậy, là điều gì đó ít thực tế hơn. Thư viện đối xử với việc đọc như một hoạt động công cộng, chia sẻ theo thời gian trong đó tôi là một trong nhiều độc giả đi qua một kệ sách cố định. Việc mua đối xử với đọc như một bộ sưu tập riêng tư, vĩnh viễn lặng lẽ đo tôi so với chính ý định của mình. Cả hai đều có giá trị, nhưng chúng định hình người đọc khác nhau. Những ngày này tôi đã bắt đầu mượn trở lại, hai cuốn một lần, cùng với những cuốn tôi sở hữu, và sự khác biệt trong cách mỗi loại sách thực sự được đọc là điều tôi vẫn đang cố hiểu một năm vào cuộc thí nghiệm.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses past perfect correctly?
options: Until I was twenty-six I have borrowed almost every book I read.
Until I was twenty-six I had borrow almost every book I read.
Until I was twenty-six I had been borrow almost every book I read.
Until I was twenty-six I had borrowed almost every book I read.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle' ('borrowed') for a habit ending at a specific past moment.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the third conditional correctly?
options: If you had asked me whether buying my own books would change my reading life, I would have said no.
If you would have asked me whether buying my own books would change my reading life, I would have said no.
If you had asked me whether buying my own books would change my reading life, I will have said no.
If you had asked me whether buying my own books would change my reading life, I would said no.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional: 'had + past participle' in the if-clause; 'would have + past participle' in the main. 'Would' can appear inside the embedded 'whether' clause.
mcQuestions.hint: 'If + had + past participle, would have + past participle'.

options: Although I was technically reading the same amount, but the experience had become heavier.
Although I was technically reading the same amount, the experience had become heavier.
Although, I was technically reading the same amount the experience had become heavier.
I was technically reading the same amount although however the experience had become heavier.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'whether… or not' correctly?
options: Return them three weeks later if I had finished them or not.
Return them three weeks later whether I had finish them or not.
Return them three weeks later whether I had finished them or not.
Return them three weeks later whether or not I had finished them is not relevant.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whether… or not' expresses indifference between two alternatives. 'If… or not' is informal; the past participle 'finished' is required.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'allow + object + to + base verb' correctly?
options: My first salary allowed me thinking of books as something I could own.
My first salary allowed me think of books as something I could own.
My first salary allowed for me to think of books as something I could own.
My first salary allowed me to think of books as something I could own.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Allow + object + to + base verb' is the standard structure. No 'for' before the object; no '-ing'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'represented + a future version of + relative clause' correctly?
options: Each one represented a future version of me who would have time, energy, and the right mood.
Each one represented a future version of me which would have time, energy, and the right mood.
Each one represented a future version of me who would has time, energy, and the right mood.
Each one represent a future version of me who would have time, energy, and the right mood.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Who' for people (a future version of me); 'would + have' (base verb). Past tense 'represented' agrees with the narrative tense.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'be told + that-clause' (passive) correctly?
options: The small humiliation of be told a book I wanted was already out.
The small humiliation of being told a book I wanted was already out.
The small humiliation of being told that a book I wanted is already out.
The small humiliation of being telling a book I wanted was already out.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Being told (that)' is the passive gerund. After a preposition ('of'), use the -ing form 'being'; tense agreement keeps 'was'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'have come to + base verb' correctly?
options: What I have come to missing is something less practical.
What I have come miss is something less practical.
What I have come to miss is something less practical.
What I have came to miss is something less practical.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have come to + base verb' for gradual realisation; past participle 'come' (not 'came').

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'a year into + noun' correctly?
options: The difference is something I am still trying to understand a year in the experiment.
The difference is something I am still trying to understand a year on the experiment.
The difference is something I am still trying to understand a year at the experiment.
The difference is something I am still trying to understand a year into the experiment.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A year into + noun' is the standard idiom meaning 'one year after starting'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'organised by + noun' correctly?
options: A shelf of unread books beside my bed, organised by intention rather than interest.
A shelf of unread books beside my bed, organised for intention rather than interest.
A shelf of unread books beside my bed, organised at intention rather than interest.
A shelf of unread books beside my bed, organising by intention rather than interest.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Organised by + noun' is the standard passive collocation for an ordering criterion.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'set deadlines, accepted my failures, and never let X + base verb'?
options: It had set deadlines, accepted my failures cheerfully, and never let unread books to accumulate as evidence against me.
It had set deadlines, accepted my failures cheerfully, and never let unread books accumulate as evidence against me.
It had set deadlines, accepted my failures cheerfully, and never let unread books accumulating as evidence against me.
It had set deadlines, accepted my failures cheerfully, and never lets unread books accumulate as evidence against me.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Let + object + base verb' (no 'to', no '-ing'). Past tense throughout: 'had set… accepted… never let'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If owning books quietly measures you against your own intentions, is that pressure useful or harmful? Should libraries be supported as a public good not just for access, but for what they let readers off the hook from?
